suggestions.name: ArrayInspector
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on inspecting and analyzing array properties without modification. They provide comprehensive array analysis capabilities including length operations, bounds checking, and sort verification. Extracting them into an ArrayInspector class separates read-only inspection concerns from array manipulation operations, improving code organization and reusability.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: analyzing and inspecting array properties without modifying the arrays. They provide information about arrays such as length retrieval, bounds checking, length comparison, and sort order verification. All methods are read-only operations that examine array characteristics.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "array inspection" domain. They collectively provide a comprehensive set of array analysis capabilities - from basic properties (length, valid indices) to more complex characteristics (sort order, length equality). They form a cohesive unit focused on answering questions about array state and properties.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Separates array inspection logic from manipulation operations
- Enhances Reusability: Inspector can be used independently by classes that only need to analyze arrays
- Reduces Complexity: Original ArrayUtils becomes more focused on array transformations
- Improves Testability: Array inspection logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate inspection concerns from manipulation concerns. This creates high cohesion (all methods inspect array properties) and low coupling (minimal dependencies, only uses basic array operations). Aligns with the Single Responsibility Principle by isolating array analysis functionality.

suggestions.name: ArrayReverser
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a highly cohesive unit focused exclusively on reversing array elements, either for entire arrays or specific ranges. Extracting them into an ArrayReverser class creates a specialized component with a single, clear responsibility that can be reused independently of the broader ArrayUtils functionality.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: reversing the order of elements in arrays. They provide two variants - reversing entire arrays and reversing specific ranges within arrays. All methods implement the same core algorithm (two-pointer swap technique) but are overloaded for different primitive types and Object arrays.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "array reversal" domain. They represent a cohesive set of operations that manipulate array element ordering through in-place reversal. The methods share identical logic patterns, parameter validation (null checks, bounds checking), and algorithmic implementation, differing only in the data types they handle.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates array reversal logic from other array utilities
- Code Reusability: Reversal functionality can be used independently across different contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 18 methods from the original ArrayUtils class, significantly reducing its size
- Testability: Reversal logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases
- Maintainability: Changes to reversal algorithm only need to be made in one place

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive group of related methods. The extraction supports the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated solely to array reversal operations. The high cohesion (all methods perform reversal) and low coupling (no dependencies on other ArrayUtils functionality) make this an ideal candidate for extraction.

suggestions.name: ArrayPrimitiveConverter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for converting wrapper object arrays to primitive arrays. They share identical conversion patterns, null-handling logic, and empty array constants. Extracting them creates a focused converter class that isolates array type conversion concerns from general array utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: converting wrapper object arrays (Boolean[], Integer[], etc.) to their corresponding primitive arrays (boolean[], int[], etc.). All methods follow the same pattern - they take a wrapper array as input, handle null cases, and return a primitive array. Some methods also provide overloads that accept a default value for null elements.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "array type conversion" domain, specifically the conversion from boxed/wrapper types to primitive types. They share common empty array constants and follow identical null-handling logic. The generic `toPrimitive(Object)` method acts as a dispatcher that delegates to the appropriate type-specific conversion method.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates array conversion logic from other array utilities
- Code Reusability: Conversion logic can be reused across different contexts beyond ArrayUtils
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 18+ conversion methods from the main ArrayUtils class, making it more focused
- Testability: Conversion logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate conversion concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all perform the same type of conversion) and low coupling (minimal dependencies on external classes). This creates a specialized converter that adheres to the Single Responsibility Principle.
